title: The three standard streams
description: stdin, stdout, and stderr are independent file descriptors (0/1/2); stdout carries data for the next stage and stderr carries diagnostics for humans.
body: Use when deciding where output should go. Every process gets fd 0 (stdin), fd 1 (stdout), fd 2 (stderr); pipes connect one program's stdout to the next's stdin, while stderr stays visible even when stdout is redirected. This is why diagnostics belong on stderr: they reach the user without polluting piped data. Do not conflate the two output streams. Actions: classify each message as data (stdout) or diagnostic (stderr). Validation signals include `tool > /dev/null` still showing errors on screen. Risk boundary: mixing diagnostics into stdout corrupts whatever consumes the pipe — the separation is functional, not stylistic.
